claims.text: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) aims to improve the data efficiency of RL by leveraging data samples collected from a source domain to facilitate learning in a similar target domain.
claims.source_quote: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) is meant to improve the data efficiency of RL by leveraging the data samples collected from a source domain to facilitate the learning in a similar target domain.

claims.text: Cross-domain transfer in RL has two fundamental and intertwined challenges: distinct state/action spaces between source and target domains requiring sophisticated inter-domain mappings, and difficulty in identifying transferability of a source-domain model a priori.
claims.source_quote: cross-domain transfer in RL is known to have two fundamental and intertwined challenges: (i) The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible and thereby requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings; (ii) The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori

claims.text: CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer because the transferability of a source-domain model is not easily identifiable a priori.
claims.source_quote: The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori, and hence CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer.

claims.text: The authors introduce the notion of cross-domain Bellman consistency as a way to measure transferability of a source-domain model.
claims.source_quote: we first introduce the notion of cross-domain Bellman consistency as a way to measure transferability of a source-domain model.

claims.text: The proposed method QAvatar combines Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.
claims.source_quote: we propose $Q$Avatar, which combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.

claims.text: The authors characterize the convergence behavior of QAvatar.
claims.source_quote: we characterize the convergence behavior of $Q$Avatar

claims.text: QAvatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.
claims.source_quote: show that $Q$Avatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.

claims.text: QAvatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation.
claims.source_quote: we demonstrate that $Q$Avatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation.

claims.text: The paper jointly tackles the challenges of distinct state/action spaces and negative transfer through cross-domain Bellman consistency and hybrid critic.
claims.source_quote: we propose to jointly tackle these two challenges through the lens of cross-domain Bellman consistency and hybrid critic.

claims.text: The code for QAvatar is publicly available at https://rl-bandits-lab.github.io/Cross-Domain-RL/.
QAvatar uses a hybrid critic approach that combines Q functions from source and target domains.
claims.source_quote: we propose $Q$Avatar, which combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.
